title: Working Effectively with Legacy Code
author: Michael C. Feathers
subtitle: Practical techniques for changing and testing untrusted codebases safely
description: The definitive guide to safely modifying code that was never designed to be tested. Feathers introduces the foundational definition — legacy code is code without tests — and delivers a systematic approach for breaking dependencies, introducing test coverage, and making changes without fear. The book covers seam identification, the Legacy Code Change Algorithm, characterization tests, and dozens of dependency-breaking refactorings (Sprout Method, Wrap Class, Extract and Override, and more). It is the essential companion to Fowler's Refactoring for anyone working on real-world codebases.
keyIdeas: Legacy code is code without tests — age and quality are secondary to testability
The Legacy Code Change Algorithm: identify change points, find test points, break dependencies, write tests, then change and refactor
A seam is a place where you can alter behavior without editing in that place — the key to breaking dependencies safely
Break dependencies for two reasons: sensing (to verify behavior) and separation (to isolate code in a test harness)
Characterization tests capture what the code actually does, not what it should do — a safety net for unknown behavior
Sprout Method and Sprout Class add new testable code alongside legacy code without touching it
Wrap Method and Wrap Class (Decorator pattern) add behavior before or after existing code without modifying it
The Legacy Code Dilemma: to change code you need tests, but to add tests you often need to change code
Scratch refactoring — refactor to understand, then revert — is a legitimate learning technique
Dependency is the root problem; most legacy work is about breaking dependencies so change becomes easier
keyTakeaways: Legacy code = code without tests. The only way to be confident in changes is to have a test safety net
The Legacy Code Change Algorithm is the canonical process: Identify → Find → Break → Write → Change
Seams are everywhere once you learn to see them — preprocessing, link, and object seams each offer different trade-offs
Break dependencies for sensing (can we observe the result?) and separation (can we run this in isolation?)
Characterization tests let you capture current behavior without knowing the specification
Sprout Method/Class let you add new functionality without modifying existing untested code
Wrap Method/Class let you insert behavior around existing code via the Decorator pattern
Parameterize Constructor and Extract and Override are the most common techniques for breaking constructor dependencies
Scratch refactoring is safe: refactor to understand the code, then throw the changes away
The goal is not perfect code — it is code that can be changed safely. Incremental improvement beats big rewrites
whoShouldRead: Every software developer who has ever been afraid to change existing code
Engineers maintaining codebases that lack test coverage
Technical leads responsible for improving codebase health and team velocity
Developers who want to add features to large, untested systems without breaking them
Anyone who has inherited a codebase and needs a systematic approach to making it safe to change
